title: Chapter 3 — Connectors, Local Files, and Permissions
CHAPTER 3 —
CONNECTORS, LOCAL FILES, AND PERMISSIONS
segments.body_html: Cowork can reach your files through several different paths: uploaded files, local folders, cloud connectors, browser ac

beats.text: Cowork can reach your files through several different paths: uploaded files, local folders, cloud connectors, browser access, direct computer use, and plugins. Each path carries a different risk profile. Granting the widest access for convenience is how things go wrong. The principle of least privilege means giving Cowork only the access the specific task needs , nothing broader, nothing extra. Before starting any task, you should be able to answer: what can Cowork read, write, send, delete, or schedule with the access I just granted?

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

segments.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO
title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS
YOU DO.
segments.body_html:
·Name each type of access Cowork can use and describe its risk profile.
·Choose the narrowest access method appropriate to a given task.
·Write an access-review prompt before starting a Cowork task.
·Know when to refuse a connector or plugin because its scope is too wide.
·Treat permissions as a routine design step, not an afterthought.

beats.text: By the end of this chapter you will be able to: Name each type of access Cowork can use and describe its risk profile. Choose the narrowest access method appropriate to a given task. Write an access-review prompt before starting a Cowork task. Know when to refuse a connector or plugin because its scope is too wide. Treat permissions as a routine design step, not an afterthought.

segments.label: 02 / 13 · WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

title: OPENING SCENE
segments.body_html:
Mariana works in grants management at a regional nonprofit. Tuesday morning she has a deadline: a funder wants a summary

Opening Scene — She does not stop to think about what "connect Google Drive" means in practice. The connector gives Cowork access to eve

beats.text: Mariana works in grants management at a regional nonprofit. Tuesday morning she has a deadline: a funder wants a summary of the last eighteen months of program reports. The files are scattered , some in a shared drive folder, some in her local downloads, one set she saved as PDFs from a portal.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · OPENING SCENE

title: THE ACCESS
LADDER.
segments.body_html:
Uploaded/task files — Bounded source packet for a single task
Local folder — Multi-file work from a prepared workspace
Cloud connector — Structured access to a specific service
Browser assistance — Public web interaction, reference lookups

beats.text: Cowork can reach work materials through several distinct paths. Think of them as a ladder: each rung gives more capability but also more exposure (Anthropic, "Use Claude Cowork safely"; Anthropic, "Let Claude use your computer in Cowork" ). Reading down the ladder is reading toward more power and more exposure. The discipline is to climb only as high as the task requires.

segments.label: 04 / 13 · THE ACCESS LADDER

segments.section: EACH ACCESS TYPE IN PLAIN TERM
title: EACH ACCESS TYPE IN
PLAIN TERMS.
segments.body_html:
The most controlled access. You choose specific files, attach them to a Cowork session, and those files become the worki

Each Access Type in Plain Terms — Use this for: one-time tasks with a clear source set. Prepare the files first (copies, not originals), attach them, and

beats.text: The most controlled access. You choose specific files, attach them to a Cowork session, and those files become the working material. Nothing else is included. When the session ends, the access ends. Use this for: one-time tasks with a clear source set. Prepare the files first (copies, not originals), attach them, and the access surface is as small as it gets.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · EACH ACCESS TYPE IN PLAIN TERM

title: THE PRINCIPLE OF
LEAST PRIVILEGE.
segments.body_html:
·What source does this task actually need?
·Can I use uploaded files instead of a folder or connector?
·If a connector, can it be scoped to a folder or project rather than the full account?
·Is browser access necessary, or can the information come from uploaded sources?
·Is computer use necessary, or is there a connector that handles this?

beats.text: The organizing principle for all of the above is least privilege: grant only the access the task requires, and nothing more (Saltzer and Schroeder 1975). This is one of the foundational rules of information security, established fifty years before agentic AI. It applies here unchanged. Least privilege is not about distrust. It is about limiting the blast radius if something goes wrong , if Cowork misinterprets an instruction, encounters an unexpected file, or gets manipulated by untrusted content. The smaller the access surface, the smaller the damage.

segments.label: 06 / 13 · THE PRINCIPLE OF LEAST PRIVILE

segments.section: ACCESS REVIEW: A PROMPT TO USE
title: ACCESS REVIEW:
A PROMPT TO USE
segments.body_html:
Before starting a task, ask Cowork to declare its access requirements. Paste this into your session:

Access Review: A Prompt to Use — "Before we begin: list exactly what access you need for this task, why each access type is necessary, what you will not

beats.text: Before starting a task, ask Cowork to declare its access requirements. Paste this into your session: "Before we begin: list exactly what access you need for this task, why each access type is necessary, what you will not touch, and what actions require my explicit approval before you proceed."

segments.label: 07 / 13 · ACCESS REVIEW: A PROMPT TO USE

segments.section: THE HUMAN GATE FOR THIS CHAPTE
title: THE HUMAN GATE FOR
THIS CHAPTER.
segments.body_html:
·What is the smallest access surface that still lets the task succeed?
·Have I scoped this to copies, a prepared folder, or a limited connector rather than my full account?
·Do I understand what actions are enabled by the access I am granting?
·Am I comfortable with what Cowork could do if it makes a reasonable error?

beats.text: The access decision is not a technical setup step. It is a judgment call that belongs to you. Cowork cannot know which files are sensitive, which cloud folder contains draft board communications, or which scheduled task would be embarrassing if it ran during an audit. You know those things. The access review is where that knowledge is applied.

segments.label: 08 / 13 · THE HUMAN GATE FOR THIS CHAPTE

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Connecting the full account because it is faster.→Assuming read-only access is safe.→Enabling a plugin without reviewing what it adds.→Using browser access for tasks that uploaded files would cover.→Scheduling high-stakes tasks without an approval gate.

beats.text: Connecting the full account because it is faster. Speed is not a reason to expand access. Prepare a task folder and upload files instead. Assuming read-only access is safe. Read access means read everything in scope. A sensitive document inside a connected folder can become source material.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · COMMON MISTAKES

title: BRIDGE TO
CHAPTER 4.
segments.body_html:
You now know what Cowork can access and how to limit it. The next question is: what do you actually ask Cowork to do?

Bridge to Chapter 4 — A vague instruction over a bounded workspace still produces unpredictable work. Chapter 4 introduces the task brief — th

beats.text: You now know what Cowork can access and how to limit it. The next question is: what do you actually ask Cowork to do? A vague instruction over a bounded workspace still produces unpredictable work. Chapter 4 introduces the task brief , the structured document that turns messy intent into an executable plan. With access scoped and a brief written, Cowork has what it needs to produce a plan worth reviewing.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · BRIDGE TO CHAPTER 4

title: AI WAYBACK
MACHINE.
segments.body_html: Dorothy Denning→Run this:→"Dorothy Denning information security"→Now make the prompt better.

beats.text: Dorothy Denning developed foundational work in information flow security , tracing how data moves between systems and what happens when that movement crosses an inappropriate boundary. Her framework maps directly onto a Cowork connector: the question is not just whether Cowork can read a document, but where that document's content flows afterward.

segments.label: 11 / 13 · AI WAYBACK MACHINE
